title: Aortic Dissection
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Critical Care
objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of acute aortic dissection
Apply Stanford and DeBakey classification systems
Differentiate Type A (surgical) from Type B (medical) management
Initiate anti-impulse therapy with appropriate BP and HR targets

vignette.text: A 62-year-old man presents with sudden severe 'tearing' chest pain radiating to back while lifting furniture. History of poorly controlled hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and 30 pack-year smoking.

physicalExam.cardiovascular: New diastolic murmur, right radial pulse stronger than left

diagnostics.CTA: Intimal flap from aortic root through descending aorta. Entry tear in ascending aorta. Small pericardial effusion.

questions.stem: Patient has sudden tearing chest/back pain, BP differential between arms, and widened mediastinum.
questions.question: What are the classic clinical features of acute aortic dissection?

options.text: Gradual onset chest pressure relieved by rest

Sudden severe chest/back pain (tearing/ripping), interarm BP differential >20 mmHg, pulse deficits, new aortic regurgitation murmur, widened mediastinum on CXR, neurologic deficits or limb ischemia from malperfusion
Pleuritic chest pain worse with inspiration
Symptoms always identical to MI

questions.explanation: Classic aortic dissection features: Sudden onset severe pain (tearing/ripping), BP differential >20 mmHg between arms, pulse deficits, new AR murmur, widened mediastinum (>8 cm). Only 15-43% correctly diagnosed at first presentation due to variable presentations.

whyChain.answer: Pain may mimic MI or PE; EKG may show ischemia from coronary malperfusion; troponin may be elevated. High suspicion needed for hypertensive patients with sudden severe pain.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Sudden severe chest/back pain (tearing/ripping), interarm BP differential >20 mmHg, pulse deficits, new aortic regurgitation murmur, widened mediastinum on CXR, neurologic deficits or limb ischemia from malperfusion.
correct.deeperWhy: Pain may mimic MI or PE; EKG may show ischemia from coronary malperfusion; troponin may be elevated. High suspicion needed for hypertensive patients with sudden severe pain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Classic aortic dissection features: Sudden onset severe pain (tearing/ripping), BP differential >20 mmHg between arms, pulse deficits, new AR murmur, widened mediastinum (>8 cm). Only 15-43% correctly

questions.stem: CTA shows dissection from aortic root through descending aorta.
questions.question: What are the Stanford and DeBakey classification systems?

options.text: All dissections are treated the same
Classifications are based on patient age
Type B is more dangerous than Type A

STANFORD: Type A = involves ascending aorta (SURGICAL EMERGENCY); Type B = does NOT involve ascending (initially MEDICAL). DEBAKEY: Type I = ascending + descending; Type II = ascending only; Type III = descending only

questions.explanation: Stanford Type A (any ascending involvement) is surgical emergency - mortality ~1-2% per hour without surgery (~50% dead by 48h). Type B (descending only) initially managed medically. This patient = Stanford Type A = DeBakey Type I.

whyChain.why: Why does Type A require surgery?
whyChain.answer: Type A risks: retrograde extension to coronary arteries (MI), aortic valve (acute AR), pericardium (tamponade). Surgery reduces mortality from ~50% to 15-25%.

sources.text: StatPearls: Aortic Dissection. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Patient has poorly controlled hypertension and smoking history.
questions.question: What are the risk factors for aortic dissection?

options.text: Hypertension is not a risk factor
All dissections are traumatic
HYPERTENSION (most common, 70%), connective tissue disorders (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, Loeys-Dietz), bicuspid aortic valve, cocaine use, extreme exertion, prior aortic surgery, pregnancy
Dissection only occurs in young patients

questions.explanation: Hypertension present in ~70% (causes medial degeneration). Marfan syndrome in 50% of patients <40 years. Acute triggers: cocaine, weightlifting, severe emotional stress. This patient: chronic HTN + acute exertion (lifting furniture).

whyChain.answer: Heritable thoracic aortic disease present in 20% of cases. First-degree relatives should undergo aortic imaging. Genetic testing recommended if <50 years or family history.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! HYPERTENSION (most common, 70%), connective tissue disorders (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, Loeys-Dietz), bicuspid aortic valve, cocaine use, extreme exertion, prior aortic surgery, pregnancy.
correct.deeperWhy: Heritable thoracic aortic disease present in 20% of cases. First-degree relatives should undergo aortic imaging. Genetic testing recommended if <50 years or family history.
correct.clinicalPearl: Hypertension present in ~70% (causes medial degeneration). Marfan syndrome in 50% of patients <40 years. Acute triggers: cocaine, weightlifting, severe emotional stress. This patient: chronic HTN + ac

questions.stem: You need to confirm the diagnosis urgently.
questions.question: What is the imaging approach for suspected aortic dissection?

options.text: Ultrasound of chest wall

CTA is gold standard (98-100% sensitivity/specificity) - shows entry tear, extent, branch involvement; TEE alternative if unstable; CXR shows widened mediastinum but neither sensitive nor specific; D-dimer elevated in >95%
Chest X-ray alone is sufficient
Cardiac catheterization is first-line

questions.explanation: CTA is imaging modality of choice - shows intimal flap, entry tear, extent, malperfusion. TEE useful bedside for unstable patients. D-dimer >500 in >95% of acute dissections - normal D-dimer helps rule out in low-probability cases.

sources.text: StatPearls: Aortic Dissection. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: While awaiting surgery, you need to stabilize this patient.
questions.question: What are the immediate medical management goals?

options.text: Target normal blood pressure
Vasodilators first, then beta-blockers
Aggressive fluid resuscitation
Anti-impulse therapy: Target SBP 100-120 mmHg AND HR <60 bpm; Start IV BETA-BLOCKER first (esmolol, labetalol) to reduce dP/dt; Add vasodilator AFTER beta-blockade if needed; Opioids for pain

questions.explanation: BETA-BLOCKER FIRST to reduce dP/dt (aortic wall stress). Vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia → increased wall stress → worse. Target SBP 100-120, HR <60. Esmolol (titratable) or labetalol preferred. Add nicardipine/nitroprusside only after HR controlled.

whyChain.answer: Vasodilators drop afterload → reflex tachycardia → increased contractility (dP/dt) → MORE wall stress → propagation/rupture. Never give nitroprusside without beta-blockade in dissection.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Anti-impulse therapy: Target SBP 100-120 mmHg AND HR <60 bpm; Start IV BETA-BLOCKER first (esmolol, labetalol) to reduce dP/dt; Add vasodilator AFTER beta-blockade if needed; Opioids for pain.

questions.stem: Cardiothoracic surgery is consulted for this Type A dissection.
questions.question: What is the surgical approach for Stanford Type A dissection?

options.text: Surgery can be delayed weeks
Medical therapy alone is preferred
EMERGENT open surgical repair: Replace ascending aorta, excise entry tear; Bentall procedure if root involved; Transfer to high-volume center if stable; Mortality 15-25% with surgery vs ~50% without
Endovascular stent is preferred for Type A

questions.explanation: Type A requires emergent open surgery. Goals: excise entry tear, replace ascending aorta, prevent tamponade/coronary occlusion. Bentall (composite valve-graft) if aortic root involved. Type B: medical unless complicated (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain) then TEVAR or surgery.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! EMERGENT open surgical repair: Replace ascending aorta, excise entry tear; Bentall procedure if root involved; Transfer to high-volume center if stable; Mortality 15-25% with surgery vs ~50% without.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.

questions.stem: CTA shows left subclavian arising from false lumen, explaining BP differential.
questions.question: What are the malperfusion syndromes in aortic dissection?

options.text: Branch vessels compromised by false lumen: CORONARY (MI), CEREBRAL (stroke), SPINAL (paraplegia), MESENTERIC (acidosis), RENAL (AKI), LIMB (pulse deficits); Malperfusion worsens prognosis
Limb ischemia never occurs
Only cardiac complications occur
Malperfusion does not affect outcomes

questions.explanation: Malperfusion from false lumen compressing branches: Coronary (RCA → inferior MI), Cerebral (stroke 5-10%), Spinal (paraplegia), Mesenteric (bowel necrosis), Renal (AKI), Limb (pulse deficits). This patient: left arm malperfusion (low BP), possible coronary (elevated troponin), possible renal (elevated creatinine).

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Branch vessels compromised by false lumen: CORONARY (MI), CEREBRAL (stroke), SPINAL (paraplegia), MESENTERIC (acidosis), RENAL (AKI), LIMB (pulse deficits); Malperfusion worsens prognosis.

questions.stem: Integrating all findings for this patient with Type A dissection.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Schedule elective surgery in 1 week
Thrombolytics for possible MI
Observe with medical therapy alone

Immediate: IV esmolol to HR <60, then nicardipine to SBP 100-120, opioids for pain; Definitive: EMERGENT surgery for ascending aortic replacement; Long-term: Lifelong BP control, serial imaging, screen first-degree relatives

questions.explanation: Type A = surgical emergency. Stabilize with anti-impulse therapy while preparing for OR. NEVER give thrombolytics (catastrophic). Post-surgery: lifelong BP control, serial imaging for residual dissection, genetic testing if young, screen relatives. 70-85% surgical survival; ~50% mortality without surgery at 48h.

whyChain.answer: ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED - would catastrophically worsen bleeding into false lumen → rupture → death. Elevated troponin is from malperfusion, not primary ACS.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected aortic dissection?

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in aortic dissection. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in aortic dissection. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in aortic dissection. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
C.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.misconception: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
D.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of aortic dissection. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

A.misconception: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

mcqs.stem: When managing aortic dissection, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

mcqs.stem: An ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.text: Right coronary artery (RCA)
options.explanation: ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads (I, aVL) localizes to an inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by RCA occlusion (~85% of cases). Check right-sided leads (V4R) to assess for RV involvement.

options.text: Left main coronary artery
options.explanation: Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.

options.text: Left anterior descending (LAD)
options.explanation: LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in V1-V4 (± V5-V6), not inferior leads.

options.text: Left circumflex (LCx)
options.explanation: LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5-V6). While LCx can cause inferior changes in ~15% of cases (dominant LCx), RCA is far more common.
